author: @richardr1891
text: If you have ever watched "Gilligan's Island" you would see similarities in your "Theme" .... six people stranded on a Deserted Tropical Island ... they made alot of stuff out of what had or what washed up ... and just so happened in one of the the first few episodes the "Professor" made batteries out of Coconuts and Fruit Juice then charged them with a Bamboo Bicycle Powered Generator ... things got interesting and funnier as time went on .... I personally met Alan Hale Jr. "The Skipper" a number of times in 1977 ... he was personal friends with the owner of a company I worked for back then and came into our boardroom a couple of times just for kicks and entertain us ... I probably make these comparisons more than most ..

author: @strongforce8466
text:
Consistency is key and you've been really consistent uploading regularly, which does have a "compounding" effect on your new subs because you have more and more videos out there so more and more new subs, so definitely do keep doing that.

Also the videos that you made that went to 100k+ I'm sure you got alot of subs from these, try to think what you did right here, the subject such as vertical wind turbine and building a battery from scratch for home energy storage, both were brilliant videos and ideas that alot of people are interested in. 

I'm sure you have alot of people that watch your videos but aren't subscribed, try to ask people to subcribe and like if they liked the video it could help, or have one of these small animations that does it for you, also activating the notification bell

The inspiration you spread could be argued by some as a countermeasure in some ways to this epidemic.
I predict about 500k in the next year, maybe even close to 1mil if you stay the course.

author: @sadaavedan
text: I came to the channel because of the battery! I have stayed on because, even though you seems to have abandoned the battery, you are now clearing up the mysteries about how different things are made, how simple a solution to a problem can be etc. The gems I have found along the way? EcoFoam; Electrolysis/HHO; Generation and storage of Hydrogen at very high efficiency, power generation from the wind, etc., etc., etc. All with the potential of becoming a serious business for those so inclined. The topics in itself are nothing new, but you take the mystery out of it and shows how it is doable. Thank you for that!

author: @garyhawkins9733
text: Can you explain, or show what you mean by actually changing your theme with youtube? Just for those that are trying to increase their own channels views and subscribers 😀

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't understand what you mean mate - you shouldn't change your theme. You can change the topic you express your theme with but the central message of your channel should always be the same. You are basically building a brand. It can take a while to understand what your brand is and a while to develop that brand but if you change your brand identity - or your theme if you like - then - that's not a good idea. But the topic changes all the time - for example a channel on fitness may do running one day, cycling the next, rowing the next - it's all about fitness but developed with different topics - running, rowing, cycling.

replies.author: @garyhawkins9733
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering ok awesome, sorry for the confusion. Thank you sir

author: @nil5221
text:
In terms of popularity and appeal to a wider audience, the results speak for themselves.
But please don't denigrate the battery days. In the way that the paper bicycle is about the stixx & all the other processes and bits & pieces, the batteries and capacitors topic was about Graphene, electrolytes, separators (remember the stamping press?), conductive inks, gravity batteries, coins, a whole host of minor topics.
It was unique, very powerful, cutting edge, you had expert knowledge to impart, like the Tech Ingredients guy.. Mate, far from running out of interest and material, I suspect there are many of that first hard-won 100k who, like myself, are quietly hoping you'll get back to it sometime, somehow, lol. At least create the library of topics and file the videos so we can easily refer back to the super-important and unique work that you did.e.g there was little video on flane-reduction of GO on a copper electrode. Can't find it.
This absolutely is NOT a criticism. It's your channel and you can do what you like, what you have fun doing.
I was just saddened to hear you talk that way about your previous 'topic' , it was scientifically ground-breaking  in a way that your more popular topics are not, and the work that you were doing was more significant. We were fascinated and I noted rumblings on other boards where others in the 'battery and capacitor' community were saying that your density claims were ridiculous, and I was waiting for you to blow them out of the water!! But it's all gone quiet. As I said, far from boring and repetitive, I thought you'd just got to the meat & bones when you stopped, left it unfinished, and moved on. Your success is proof of the wisdom of that move, but man, I wish that there was still some content in that area.
Anyway, one minor request: in an "Early Days" file, could you leave up a couple of those first blurry, scratchy, wobbly old videos of the younger you mixing up graphene in jars? Just for historical interest, as an example to other YT Channellers of what's possible.  Great days :-) that developed into a very weighty and significant body of work, uniquely explained to the viewer. We learned so much and I think the information you gave will have a bigger impact on the world than the lighter-weight but less "niche/nerdy" topics that you now cover. Personally, I now find it more entertaining rather than fascinatingly informative, but that's me.
Onwards and  upwards. I look forward every day to waking up (Australia) and first thing, get a coffee and watch your latest.

author: @mrdewilliams
text:
The additional element that makes your channel popular is one that viewers may not realize affects their own viewership, but upon reflection will probably agree with.  You talk TO your viewers, not AT them.  You come across as if I, your viewer, am sitting right across that table from you.  Your viewers feel like they are a part of your channel, because you make them feel like they are part of it.  Just talking AT your viewers just leaves them feeling like viewers.  I watch your videos and see a guy I would love to have as a neighbor on my block.

So, see you later, neighbor.

author: @cruesome1971
text:
I agree with all of your points that you've made here, but I think that you're ignoring one very important and valuable component and that's you, your ability to teach people who know absolutely nothing about your topic, and your knowledge in the field.

Your DIY projects are very interesting to watch and learn from because you begin with the curiosity that we all begin with -- is this going to work or not -- and you show us the process of trial and error until that thing succeeds.

People like to be included in the process and not just shown how to build projects.

author: @chrishayes5755
text: I'm an online marketer and can give you a few simple pointers to get to 1M subs much faster: every video intro and outro you should remind your viewers to subscribe and share to help the channel grow. do content syndication. this means when you create a new video (and it's exceptionally good or important) you manually go out and share it in various high traffic forums, blogs etc. for example reddit, eevblog. this not only gets you direct traffic but also encourages youtubes algorithm to boost the ranking of your video. there are many forums where you can have a link in your signature, if you post often that can get you tens of thousands of clicks per month to your channel that way too. there are lots more strategies but that's the low hanging fruit :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thank you mate - I will try it - cheers

author: @3deeguy
text: Growing a channel requires 'insight' which I see as a talent. It's not a talent that I have. If I had a large audience I think I would be 'preoccupied' with making missteps.

author: @bigsteve6729
text: Robert I work in SEO and traffic driving to websites and often see that once a website reaches so many followers or viewers, it often has a snowball effect, think people in a market rushing to a stall, if there's 100000 people at the stall already when you walk past, it's really likely you'll want to stick around for a bit to see what's going on. You will probably see this effect at each big milestone reaching it in a shorter amount of time as long as your content is fresh and on topic! You also engage with your audience which YouTube loves. Congratulations. Now you know your niche how about a concept for a different more professional series as a different feature on the channel? Think something like, so you're plane has crashed on an island, here's all the parts you can salvage from a plane, how comfortable can you make your island from the parts you find or what is washed up. 10 episodes of different ideas to harness nature for power/water/food when you have nothing. Definitely give it a go now you have some momentum behind you. Give us a shout if you need more ideas, love the videos by you and Luke as I'm a fervent tinkerer myself!

Choose a path with heart and employ the gifts we are given. Good day to you mate. Cheers! :))

author: @ClericChris

author: @Starche_fpv
text:
Hey Rob, I’m convinced you’ll get your 1M subscribers quite soon just because, as it’s said, you’re being bloody good :)
Really happy to see your progress, though I miss those battery videos. You made me find or buy a bunch of graphite rods, acids, alkalis, foils, wires, ultrasonic cleaner… and actually encouraged me to experiment which was quite fun. I can’t really describe how happy I was when a 2x5 piece of foil and carbon ran a motor for 15 minutes! Thanks for the experience, really.

author: @BiancaAguglia
text: YouTube recommended me one of your videos after I had watched several other videos about DIY wind turbines. That's how I found your channel. I stayed here because I like not only the kind of projects your work on, but also your personality, the many nuggets of wisdom, the thinly veiled contempt for mediocrity and for lack of personal responsibility, and your general attitude towards life. Keep up the great work. 😊

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text: Rob has a so much enthusiasm and passion, and a bloody fantastic sense of humour as well!😁❤️AX

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text:
Is it a contempt for "mediocrity", or a contempt for thinking there exists such a thing as "mediocrity"?  
I tend to think he celebrates the "ordinary" as being anything BUT "ordinary".  😊

replies.author: @BiancaAguglia
replies.text:
​@NorthernKitty Good point, but my comment might make more sense if I added a few words. 🙂See, one way I define mediocrity is settling for less than your best. When I say "best", I don't mean this ridiculous race to be wealthy, famous, or in a so called respected job. By best, I mean taking the time to find what's meaningful to you and putting your best effort into making it happen or making it work. 

By many of today's standards, there's nothing "extraordinary" about Rob. He's an "ordinary" person, in a workshop that's probably less "ordinary" (if you think of how many people have such a workspace) but close to "ordinary" when you think of the kinds of tools and materials he uses. Through a combination of passion and effort, Rob manages to change his "ordinary" means into an "extraordinary" adventure. And he does so while staying (mostly 😁) laid-back, witty, and fun.

Other people might come up with all sorts of excuses about why they can't work on things that make them happy or make them fulfilled. To me, that's mediocrity: settling for excuses instead on giving life your best shot. That's what I meant when I talked about Rob's contempt.

I fully agree with what you said about celebrating the "ordinary". Most of the videos I've watched on his channel are just that: a celebration of the "extraordinary ordinary". 😊

author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
text: I’ll tell you what I think as it’s why I’ve ended up watching you virtually every day……you do ‘stuff’ that I’m interested in (most of what you do) and you have the drive/motivation to work out/try out builds and experiments. In other words you save me (and others I presume) the hassle of failure (as you work out what does/doesn’t work) and show what can be done with, sometimes, little effort and money. However I do so wish you’d make a huge battery bank and charge it with solar panels and run a house off of it…….yeah, me and 200k others too I expect!. Anyway thanks for what you do and the entertainment. 😀👍🏴󠁧󠁢󠁷󠁬󠁳󠁿

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - but to a degree that expectation is just too high - I don't have the money for it

replies.author: @aadhithvarathan5432
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I am sure you will be able to find a super cheap high energy density battery that can do this. In India, whenever there is a current cut, a 12V 60Ah Lead Acid battery that is 50cm by 50 cm by 10cm can power a house for 9 hours before flatting. I am sure Lithium, Aluminium and Carbon batteries are more superior to this.

replies.author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yes, but, it’s the ultimate prize for lots of us as we all dream of going off-grid and in that regard I, for one, would be happy to help share the costs involved and I’d be very surprised if others WOULDN’T want to either. It would be fantastic if someone like you could show/prove that it was possible to do and beat the big and expensive boys. I know it’s perhaps a whim but if anyone could do it I’d put your name forward in a heartbeat. Don’t get me wrong I really like the way you progress through the stuff you do, I’m simply fantasising about the time I open up a new vid and you say you want to construct an off grid bank but need help (patreons?) to do it……way hey! 👍

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Pish. No offense meant. You've made monster sized supercaps before (sorry EESDs) out of a ream of printer-paper. How hard/expensive could it be to spray a roll of news-print with ink etc and swiss-roll it with turkey foil to get a humongous storage? (obviously concentric layers to get the voltage up). That and your 'rose' and/or other energy-capture devices could charge it/them. Put together an inverter out of 2 MOTs. Bingo. Maybe Luke would like to try putting a demo system out of some of them.
Your wonderful devices are great inspiration and take a lot of the 'it's rocket-science' mystery out of the normally hidden fundamentals; but how about putting several into a combining project (even if it's just a diagram labelled e.g .'see video 956, 1121, etc' for reference)?  Then stand back and watch viewers/subscribers go at it.
You don't need to replace 'the grid', but lighting and laptop/tv power etc, maybe microwave/kettle (for short periods) should really grab some viewer attention. Maybe even run 'a welder'  ;-)  Or charge the electric assist/powered bike/trike.
I'm interested in the idea of home-built sterling engine/generators and combining that with solar-concentrator/heat store ( a cubic metre of rain water hold lots of energy); I used curved shower-screens for concentrators focused onto old pipe inside fluorescent light tubes, but it's easy to make trough or dish reflectors. And cheap/free.   If only skip-diving were an Olympic sport...   Sigh.
Thanks for sharing. It's much appreciated.
P.S. Would a solar panel manufacturer maybe donate some, and see what you can do with them and your 'ink' EESDs ideas?   e.g. Colin Furze got a Ford van donated. I'm not talking 'sponsored' because you've shown labelled products before.  But.. y'know. ?
Ciao.

author: @mySeaPrince_
text:
I found you because of taking a battery apart...
as I wanted to repair a 220ah as only one cell had gone bad..
Then there was the subject of EFB and Carbon bagged Negative plate LA batteries...
I've been off grid for over 30 years...
Thank you for your channel...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I do like my batteries mate lol !

replies.author: @mySeaPrince_
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering 
The EFB is a valuable move forward that can be purchased in the UK... 200 cycles at 50%DoD .. more at 30%DoD

Then there's bagging the negative plate..? In effect a Super Capacitor Lead Acid Battery lasting well over 5000 cycles..
... funny can't seem to buy them in UK.. wonder why?
 if people look up Ultra Battery Australia or UltraBattery - CSIRO
... Could you do a proper how to repair or modify a LA battery video?

I agree totally about theme / subject..

There was another difference that I think happened.. you gave answers / end product..
Or at least showing the practical example results ie wind turbines..
and making things out of other things.. been doing it all my life.. oh the joys of the skip..

... rather than experimentation...
You showed the principles of making a battery ...
But...
ie .. making a cake..
flour, egg, sugar...
but no quantities or cooking times..

And rule 1 of making a wind turbine.. don't test blades rotating on a screwdriver...
rule 2 there will be no wind for weeks after you put up turbine...

Regards Mark.
